bull_thesis: ALHG.PA is Louis Hachette Group (not Albin Michel, as the peer file mislabels it) — a holding company controlled by Bollore that owns 66.53% of Lagardere SA (home to Hachette Livre, the world's #3 trade book publisher, and Lagardere Travel Retail, the global #2 airport-concession operator) and 100% of Prisma Media. The bull case treats this as a sum-of-the-parts trade (valuing each business line separately) on a holding company that trades below the value of what it owns, where the underlying businesses are genuinely good and are paying down debt. For the full year 2025 (reported 19 Feb 2026): group revenue was EUR9.62bn (up 3.3% on a like-for-like basis), adjusted EBITA was EUR551M (up 8%), net profit doubled to EUR112M, net debt was cut by EUR236M to EUR1.59bn, and economic leverage (net debt relative to earnings) fell from 2.4x to 1.95x, with EUR800M refinanced (a EUR500M five-year bond oversubscribed three times plus a EUR300M private placement, weighted-average maturity of 2.9 years). That makes the screener's Altman-Z score of 1.1 — a 'distress' flag — a false positive, driven by counting low-margin travel-retail revenue against gross debt. The crown jewel, Lagardere Travel Retail, posted record EBITDA of EUR312M (up 17%, a 5.1% operating margin) on revenue up 5.5% to EUR6.1bn, and won the Schiphol duty-free contract — a structural beneficiary of the post-COVID travel recovery, protected by a moat built on regulatory licenses and scale concessions, the opposite of a business in secular decline. Hachette Livre grew 3% like-for-like in a flat print market (Asterix sold more than 2m copies). The first-ever dividend (EUR0.06, roughly a 3.5% yield) signals confidence in returning capital. On a look-through basis — valuing the company by its share of what it owns — the 66.53% Lagardere stake is worth about EUR1.76-1.97bn, plus roughly EUR0.1-0.22bn of Prisma, for about EUR1.86-2.19 per share gross, against the EUR1.71 share price. So the equity is modestly cheap relative to its parts and carries upside from two sources: (1) Lagardere re-rating as travel retail compounds and leverage keeps falling, and (2) an eventual structural simplification (buying in the Lagardere minority holders or collapsing to a single clean listing) that would close the double holding-company discount. Analyst consensus is Strong Buy (4 of 4) with a EUR2.30 target (about 34% upside). This rests on a few conditions: travel demand stays resilient, deleveraging continues, and the controlling shareholder eventually simplifies the structure on terms that do not shortchange minority holders.
bear_thesis: ALHG is a minority stub sitting under two layers of discount inside a vehicle that Bollore controls (with a 31% stake), and its only real upside catalyst — structural simplification — has not been announced, is entirely at the controller's discretion, and is tangled up in active Vivendi/AMF/EU litigation. The apparent cheapness against net asset value is far thinner than it looks once you use the correct share count of roughly 1bn. At EUR1.71 on about 1bn shares (a EUR1.71bn market cap), ALHG trades at only an 8-22% discount to the gross look-through net asset value — a normal discount for a controlled holding company, not a deep mispricing — and that discount is structurally justified. Value leaks away twice (from Louis Hachette Group down to the 33.5% Lagardere minority holders at MMB.PA, then down to the operating subsidiaries), and minority shareholders cannot force any action. The governance overhang is live and unfavorable: in April 2025 the Paris Court of Appeal found the Bollore family had exercised effective control and ordered the AMF (the French market regulator) to reconsider whether a mandatory buyout was owed at the Vivendi split; the EU gun-jumping probe went against the companies at the General Court on 3 June 2026, with Vivendi appealing to the CJEU (the EU's top court) — an open-ended legal coin-flip that could just as easily impose costs or force a transaction on bad terms as deliver a clean buy-in. The related-party purchase of the Vivendi/Prisma stake (closed 31 March 2026) hints that the controller may recombine Bollore-complex assets on terms that favor itself. The economics that actually reach ALHG shareholders are thin (attributable net income of about EUR22M versus EUR112M at the group level; a 0.2% net margin), because of minority dilution and a low-margin mix. Prisma is a shrinking, loss-making magazine business (revenue down 9% to EUR266M, EBITDA of negative EUR43M) in the tail end of print decline. The price sits at the 84th percentile of its 52-week range and above its 200-day moving average — so the modest cheapness is already partly recognized, and the EUR2.30 target needs both a Lagardere re-rating and a discount-closing event.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name, the usual question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into earnings that are actually stable. Here it is the reverse. The Strong-Buy consensus (4 of 4 buys, a EUR2.30 target, about 34% upside) is arguably too optimistic — it appears to price in both a Lagardere re-rating and a holding-company-discount-closing simplification as if either had a date attached. The screener's own framing is also wrong: it treats ALHG as a distressed, thin-margin book publisher (Altman-Z of 1.1, a 0.2% net margin, price-to-free-cash-flow of 1.66x), which is an artifact of misidentifying the company. The real entity is a deleveraging holding company (economic leverage of 1.95x, a bond oversubscribed three times) whose attributable margin is thin only because 33.5% of the best assets leak to Lagardere minority holders. The genuinely mispriced perception is the 'distress' reading — but correcting it lands the stock near fair value relative to net asset value, not deeply cheap. There is no large consensus error to exploit in either direction once the entity is correctly identified: the gap to the EUR2.30 target is a bet on a re-rating, not a mispricing the market has wrong today.
forcing_function: There is no hard, dated catalyst here. The load-bearing one — structural simplification (buying in the Lagardere minority holders, or moving to a single clean listing) that would collapse the double holding-company discount — has not been announced and is entirely at the discretion of the Bollore controller; minority shareholders cannot force it. The dated events that do exist are litigation, not value-unlock: the CJEU appeal of the 3 June 2026 EU General Court loss (a multi-quarter, two-sided process) and the AMF mandatory-buyout review (open-ended). The Prisma/Vivendi clean-up has already happened (closed 31 March 2026, its re-rating spent, related-party, about EUR30M — immaterial). So the only true catalyst is a corporate action with no date on the controller's clock — the very definition of a soft, repeatedly-deferrable catalyst.
valley_of_death: Three near-term (3-9 month) risks define the risky window just ahead. (1) Travel-demand / oil tail: more than 60% of revenue is Lagardere Travel Retail, which is tied to global air traffic; the current environment carries a live oil/Hormuz tail (WTI around EUR92, inflation re-accelerating). Any escalation that cuts air traffic would hit the single best segment directly, and a renewed spike in the VIX (the market's volatility index) would mechanically widen the holding-company discount. (2) Litigation timing: the EU gun-jumping appeal to the CJEU and the AMF mandatory-buyout review can produce headline-driven drawdowns at unscheduled dates, with the risk skewed to the downside (costs imposed, or a forced transaction on bad terms). (3) Prisma drag: continued restructuring charges (about 300 jobs, EBITDA of negative EUR43M) keep group attributable earnings thin. There is no wall of debt maturities (weighted-average maturity of 2.9 years, just refinanced) — solvency is not the danger here. The danger is a demand or discount-widening air-pocket before any simplification is even announced.
positioning_washout: There is little forced-selling pressure in play. ALHG is a recently-listed (December 2024) Euronext Growth name with a roughly 60% free float, but with a 31% Bollore control block and 8.6% held by Arnaud Lagardere — it is not a yield-driven, passive-heavy holder base whose liquidation a dividend cut would force (the dividend was just initiated, not cut). The cleaner risk runs the other way: as a small, thinly-covered Growth-segment stub, an adverse litigation headline or a travel-demand scare could trigger outsized selling by retail and momentum holders (the price sits at the 84th percentile of its 52-week range, leaving it exposed to mean-reversion), and any index or liquidity reshuffle would be lumpy. There is no forced-seller tailwind here and no capitulation already in the price to buy into — the holder base is controller-anchored and stable, which removes both the washout risk and the washout opportunity.
moderator_conclusion: Pass for now, conviction 2. First, the identity correction is decisive: ALHG.PA is Louis Hachette Group, a Bollore-controlled holding company sitting over 66.53% of Lagardere plus 100% of Prisma — not the thin, distressed book publisher the screener and peer file describe. So the deep-value signal (price-to-free-cash-flow of 1.66x, Altman-Z distress) is an artifact and must be discarded. Correctly identified, this is an asset-backed special situation trading below the value of its parts, with two genuinely good operating businesses that are paying down debt (Travel Retail, the #2 player, at record EBITDA; Hachette, the #3 publisher) and real balance-sheet improvement (leverage of 1.95x, a bond oversubscribed three times) — a fundamentally sound name, not a value trap. But the trade fails the regime test on two counts. (i) The catalyst is absent: the only discount-closing event is an undated, controller-discretionary simplification — exactly the soft, single-binary, third-party-controlled catalyst (with no contractual payout) that the regime deprioritizes — while the one event that has already happened (Prisma/Vivendi, 31 March 2026) is spent and immaterial. (ii) Independence of the drivers fails: the upside leans on the Bollore/Vivendi/AMF/CJEU governance complex (a factor shared across the whole group, not edge specific to LHG), and the best segment is directly exposed to the regime's live oil/Hormuz air-traffic tail — two risk drivers that move together rather than independently, which the discipline tells us to penalize. On the corrected share count of roughly 1bn, the stock trades essentially at its look-through net asset value (a base sum-of-the-parts of about EUR1.75 versus the EUR1.71 price; roughly 1.3-to-1 reward versus risk at the extremes), so there is no deep margin of safety (cushion against being wrong) to justify waiting, and the price is already at the 84th percentile of its range. The verdict is pass for now, conviction 2: a quality holding company at a fair-to-modest discount, with no dated catalyst and shared-factor upside — one to watch for a genuine simplification announcement, or a travel/oil-shock flush toward EUR1.30-1.40 (down toward the bearish net asset value) that would restore real asymmetry, but no capital today. Separately, the value conviction is 3 (not 2): judged purely on valuation and forensic quality, with no catalyst or regime overlay, this is a sound, deleveraging collection of real assets with a narrow but stable moat, trading near net asset value — that is fairly-valued quality (a legitimate 3), not the 1-2 of a melting value trap. The moat is narrow and stable and the secular threat is manageable (Prisma is in terminal decline but is only about 3% of revenue and is being disposed of; Travel Retail is a recovery asset, not a secular loser), so no eroding-moat or terminal-threat cap applies. The two scores diverge correctly: conviction 2 (no catalyst, fails the regime test) versus value conviction 3 (fair-value quality on the parts).
sop_breakdown: Base-case look-through net asset value on roughly 1,001M shares: the 66.53% of Lagardere SA at a blended valuation of about EUR2.8bn works out to roughly EUR1.86 per share; 100% of Prisma, marked conservatively at about EUR0.12bn, is roughly EUR0.12 per share; gross net asset value is therefore about EUR1.98 per share. Applying a roughly 12% base discount for a controlled holding company (justified by value leaking twice to the MMB.PA minority holders plus the live governance and litigation overhang, partly offset by the genuine deleveraging and the simplification optionality) gives a base sum-of-the-parts fair value of about EUR1.75. The holding-company-level net debt is already captured inside the Lagardere stake (the EUR1.59bn of economic net debt sits at Lagardere); the screener's EUR4.49bn is the fully-consolidated figure, and it is a liability embedded in the equity value of the stake, not something added back on top. The fair value sits essentially at the EUR1.71 market price — the stock is fairly valued to modestly cheap, not deeply mispriced, once the correct share count is used.
risk_reward: Roughly symmetric, and unattractive against the asymmetry this book demands. The upside to a bullish fair value (EUR2.25) is about 32%; the downside to a bearish fair value (EUR1.30) is about 24%; the base fair value (EUR1.75) is roughly flat to up 2%. That is about 1.3-to-1 reward versus risk at the extremes, but the upside is gated by an undated, controller-discretionary catalyst, while the downside (a widening discount plus a travel-demand/oil shock plus an adverse ruling) can fire at any time. Net: a thin, timing-uncovered 1.3-to-1 — not the asymmetry the book requires.
peer_comps_note: The bundled peer-groups file is invalid for this name. It mislabels ALHG.PA as 'Albin Michel, a French trade book publisher' and benchmarks it against Lagardere, Mondadori, Schibsted, and Scholastic on price-to-free-cash-flow and book-publishing economics. ALHG.PA is Louis Hachette Group, a holding company owning 66.53% of Lagardere plus 100% of Prisma — so the correct 'peer' is its own underlying (Lagardere SA / MMB.PA, with a market cap of about EUR2.65-2.96bn) on a look-through net-asset-value basis, not a print-publisher multiple. The relevant relative-value lever is the holding-company discount to net asset value (about 8-22% gross), which is normal for a controlled Bollore vehicle, not idiosyncratic edge. The Vivendi/Lagardere governance-litigation overhang is a factor shared across the Bollore complex (a sector/regulatory-style convergence), not a name-specific edge — the discount is largely justified, and the residual mispricing is modest. Do not credit the print-publisher cheapness (price-to-free-cash-flow of 1.66x) from the peer file; it is an artifact of the misidentification.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — ALHG.PA (Louis Hachette Group S.A.)

**Critical identity correction:** the screener and peer file label ALHG.PA as "Albin Michel, a French trade book publisher." This is wrong. ALHG.PA is Louis Hachette Group S.A. (LHG) — a holding company controlled by Bollore, spun out of Vivendi in October 2024 and listed on Euronext Growth Paris on 16 December 2024. LHG owns 66.53% of Lagardere SA (which itself houses Lagardere Publishing, i.e. Hachette Livre, and Lagardere Travel Retail) plus 100% of Prisma Media. This is not a thin, single-line book publisher; it is a three-segment media and travel-retail conglomerate with EUR9.6bn of revenue, held through a holding-company structure. Every downstream number — the EUR9.23B-to-EUR9.62B revenue jump, the multi-billion net debt, the 0.2% net margin — is explained by fully consolidating Lagardere, not by a book publisher. The entire peer-group rationale ("structurally thin economics of physical book publishing," Altman-Z distress) is built on a misidentified company and must be discarded.

No FMP earnings-call transcript was bundled. This dossier is built from the full-year 2025 results press release and earnings call (reported 19 Feb 2026), the Prisma transaction release (closed 31 March 2026), the first-half 2025 release, Lagardere SA disclosures, and the Vivendi/Lagardere litigation record — all web-sourced and dated below.

## 1. Narrative arc

Since its December 2024 listing, LHG's story has been a deleveraging-and-simplification narrative wrapped around a cyclical recovery in two strong operating businesses. At listing the pitch was "a leading, diversified player in publishing, travel retail and media" — a holding company housing Lagardere's two crown jewels (Hachette Livre, the world's #3 trade publisher; Lagardere Travel Retail, the global #2 airport and travel concession operator) plus a structurally-declining magazine business (Prisma).

By full-year 2025 the emphasis had visibly shifted from "diversified holding company" to "deleverage and clean up the portfolio." Management led with: (i) net-debt reduction of EUR236M to EUR1,590M and leverage down from 2.4x to 1.95x; (ii) the Prisma luxury-division disposal and Vivendi's purchase of a 13.58% Prisma-holding stake, framed by Arnaud Lagardere as "refocus[ing] its portfolio on its core historical businesses"; (iii) Travel Retail's record EBITDA (EUR312M, up 17%) and the Schiphol duty-free contract takeover. The driver that was quietly contained rather than dropped is Prisma — repeatedly described in defensive, restructuring language ("downturn in business," "rapidly changing market," about 300 jobs cut across two plans, EBITDA of negative EUR43M). The arc is therefore stabilizing-to-strengthening at the group level (deleverage on track, two healthy segments) with one segment in managed decline.

## 2. Claims versus financials (forensic)

| Management claim (full-year 2025 call, 19 Feb 2026) | Hard evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "Revenue EUR9.6bn, +4% reported / +3.3% like-for-like" | Screener: revenue EUR9.62B versus EUR9.23B in FY24 = +4.2% year over year. The 3-year "CAGR +210.9%" is a demerger/consolidation artifact (2023 showed EUR979M as a stub; full Lagardere consolidation lands in FY24). True organic run-rate is about +3-4%. | Match — and it confirms the 210% growth rate is noise, not acceleration. |
| "Adjusted EBITA EUR551M, +8%" | Implies about a 5.7% group operating margin — thin because EUR6.1bn of revenue is low-margin travel retail (a 5.1% operating margin) and EUR266M is loss-making Prisma. The screener's 0.2% net margin reflects minority interests (33.5% of Lagardere leaks to MMB.PA minority holders) plus interest on consolidated debt. | Match — the optically "near-zero net margin" is a holding-company/minority-leakage artifact, not operational distress. |
| "Net profit more than doubled to EUR112M (from EUR52M)" | The screener's net income of EUR22M is the slice attributable to LHG after minorities; EUR112M is the group figure. Both can be true. | Match, with a caveat: equity holders of ALHG see a thin slice; minority holders (the Lagardere free float) capture the rest. |
; "Net debt EUR1.59bn, leverage 1.95x (from 2.4x)"; The screener's "net debt EUR4.49B" is the fully-consolidated Lagardere balance sheet (total debt of EUR5.12bn minus EUR633M of cash). The EUR1.59bn is LHG's economic/covenant leverage measure. Both are real; the sum-of-the-parts must use the consolidated enterprise value but credit the deleveraging trajectory.; Reconciled — the gap is consolidation scope, not a hidden liability. In any sum-of-the-parts, net debt is a liability to subtract.; 
| Travel Retail "record EBITDA EUR312M, +17%; 5.1% operating margin; Schiphol takeover seamless" | Consistent with Lagardere SA's own FY25 (Travel Retail revenue up 5.5% to EUR6,133M). A genuine post-COVID travel recovery. | Match — the strongest part of the story. |
| Publishing "Hachette #3 in US; Asterix >2m copies; +3% like-for-like in a declining market" | Print is structurally flat-to-down; +3% like-for-like on a EUR3.0bn base is share gain on the back of frontlist hits. Gross margin "contracting" (45.6%) is the print-cost tell. | Match but fragile — hit-driven, low-visibility revenue. |
| Dividend EUR0.06/share proposed | First dividend as a listed company; about a 3.5% yield at EUR1.71. Modest, consistent with "deleverage first." | Match |

The narrative is honestly poorer than the holding-company optics suggest and honestly richer than the screener's "0.2% net margin / Altman-Z distress" optics suggest — both distortions cancel once you see the consolidation structure.

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

Rising/stable. As a freshly-listed holding company there are only about 2-3 reporting cycles, but the trajectory from first-half 2025 to full-year 2025 is one of growing specificity and confidence on deleverage and Travel Retail, paired with increasingly defensive, surgical language on Prisma. Full-year 2025 management was concrete: named leverage targets (1.95x, with continued de-gearing), specific refinancing (a EUR500M five-year bond oversubscribed three times plus a EUR300M private placement, weighted-average maturity of 2.9 years), and forward guidance ("stable 2026 publishing revenue," "mid-single-digit travel-retail growth," margins consistent). The shift from offensive (growth/M&A) to defensive (cost/restructuring) language is isolated to Prisma, not the group. Net: confidence stable-to-rising, with management demonstrably willing to give dated, falsifiable guidance.

## 4. Guidance credibility

Medium-high, but a short track record. LHG has guided only a handful of times since listing; full-year 2025 delivered the deleveraging it promised at the half-year (net debt down EUR236M, leverage from 2.4x to 1.95x — concrete and verifiable). Travel Retail hit "record" numbers consistent with parent Lagardere's disclosures. There is no multi-year history to test for sandbagging, and the attributable earnings-per-share line is so thin and minority-diluted that small swings look large (the latest quarter's earnings per share, down 70% year over year on the screener, is noise on a near-zero base, not a miss). Reliability: medium (it would be high with another 2-3 cycles). The credibility risk is not management's honesty on operations — it is the governance/control overhang (see sections 5 and 6) that sits above the operating story.

## 5. Analyst pressure points

The recurring, unresolved questions are not operational — they are structural and about governance:
1. **The holding-company discount and simplification path.** LHG trades at a discount to its look-through net asset value (66.53% of Lagardere is about EUR1.85-1.97bn at Lagardere's roughly EUR2.96bn market cap, plus 100% of Prisma, versus LHG's own roughly EUR1.2bn market cap). Analysts press on whether and when the two-tier Lagardere/LHG structure collapses (a merger, a buy-in of Lagardere minority holders, or a simplified single listing). Management has not committed to a timeline — this is the load-bearing, unanswered question.
2. **The Vivendi-demerger legal cloud.** Analysts probe the AMF/court overhang: the Paris Court of Appeal (April 2025) found the Bollore family exercised effective control and ordered the AMF to reconsider whether a mandatory buyout offer was owed at the Vivendi split; separately, the EU gun-jumping probe into Vivendi/Lagardere went against the companies at the General Court (3 June 2026), with Vivendi appealing to the CJEU. Management deflects these as Vivendi/parent matters.
3. **Prisma's terminal value.** Analysts keep returning to whether Prisma is a permanent EBITDA drag; management's answer is disposals plus a digital/AI pivot, not a fix.

## 6. Red flags / green flags

Green: deleveraging is real and on track (net debt down EUR236M, 2.4x to 1.95x; EUR800M refinanced, weighted-average maturity of 2.9 years, no near-term maturity wall) — the Altman-Z "1.1 distress" flag is a false positive driven by counting low-margin travel-retail revenue against gross debt; it does not reflect refinancing risk.
Green: two genuinely good operating assets — Lagardere Travel Retail (global #2, record EBITDA, structural post-COVID tailwind) and Hachette Livre (world's #3 trade publisher, gaining share).
Green: first dividend initiated (EUR0.06) — a capital-return signal, not a cut.
Green: portfolio clean-up underway — the Prisma luxury sale closed, non-core trimmed.
Red: holding-company-on-holding-company structure — value leaks twice (LHG to Lagardere minority holders to operating subsidiaries); 33.5% of the best assets accrues to MMB.PA holders, not ALHG.
Red: governance/control overhang — Bollore's 31% control plus Arnaud Lagardere's contested position plus the AMF mandatory-buyout question plus the EU gun-jumping appeal add up to an open-ended legal/governance binary the equity cannot dictate.
Red: Prisma is a structurally shrinking, loss-making segment (revenue down 9% to EUR266M, EBITDA of negative EUR43M) in the cord-cutting/print-decline tail.
Red: Euronext Growth listing, thin-float optics — minority equity in a controlled vehicle; the controller, not the market, sets the simplification clock.

## 7. Hidden signals

- The language migration around Prisma — from "media" to "refocus on core historical businesses," "digital transformation," "generative AI" — is the standard euphemism arc for managing a melting asset toward disposal, not investing into it. Watch for a full Prisma exit.
- The purchase of Vivendi's 13.58% Prisma-holding stake is a related-party tell: it monetizes a sliver of Prisma to a Bollore-affiliated counterparty (Vivendi) and hints the controller may recombine assets across the Bollore complex over time — a double-edged signal for minority holders (it could simplify, or it could be done on terms favoring the controller).
- Management's repeated emphasis on covenant leverage (1.95x) rather than consolidated gross debt is a deliberate framing to steer analysts away from the headline EUR4.5bn consolidated figure — legitimate, but a framing choice worth noting.
- The EUR500M bond oversubscribed three times is a real credit-market vote of confidence that contradicts the screener's distress flag.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

Classification: stable to strengthening at the operating level; the equity story is a controlled holding-company-discount special situation whose resolution clock is held by the Bollore family, not the market.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: this is a holding-company-discount / sum-of-the-parts trade, not an operating-momentum trade — and the catalyst that would close the discount (structural simplification, a Lagardere minority buy-in, or a single clean listing) has not been announced, is at the controller's discretion, and is entangled with active Vivendi/AMF/EU litigation. The underlying assets (Travel Retail, the #2 player; Hachette, the #3 publisher) are real and the deleveraging is genuine, so the downside is cushioned by tangible net asset value; but the upside requires a corporate action the minority cannot force, on a timeline the controller will set to suit itself. Size it as a patient, asset-backed position with low conviction on timing — never as a dated-catalyst bet.

Economic moat: Lagardere Travel Retail has a genuine moat built on regulatory licenses and scale-cost concessions (long-dated airport contracts, global #2 scale, switching costs for airport authorities) — narrow-to-decent and currently widening (record EBITDA, the Schiphol win). Hachette Livre has an intangible-brand/backlist moat (Asterix, Twilight, frontlist relationships) — narrow and roughly stable in a flat print market. Prisma has no moat and is structurally impaired. Blended at the group level: a narrow moat, trend stable (Travel Retail widening offsets Prisma erosion). This is not a high-but-falling-returns eroding-moat situation; group returns on invested capital are low-but-recovering off the COVID trough, which is different.

Secular threat: the print/magazine secular decline (cord-cutting/print) is terminal for Prisma, but Prisma is only about 3% of revenue and is already loss-making and being disposed of — so at the group level the secular threat is manageable, not terminal. Travel Retail (more than 60% of revenue) is a post-COVID recovery asset with a structural travel-growth tailwind, the opposite of a secular loser. The cheapness here is therefore best read as a holding-company discount plus a governance-overhang fear-print on sound assets, not a structurally-shrinking base — that is, closer to mispricing than to a value trap, gated by the absence of a forcing catalyst.

Management credibility scores a 4, the trajectory is strengthening, the moat is narrow, the moat trend is stable, and the secular threat is manageable.
